<!-- Generated by the ccode-to-codex mirror sync on 2026-04-14 from input revision 9a2403fb. -->

# Sub-Agent Migration Guide: Claude Agent()/Task() -> Codex Custom Agents

This guide covers the part that pure workflow migration does not: converting
embedded Claude sub-agent sites into Codex-native delegation patterns.

Official grounding:

- OpenAI Codex sub-agents guide: <https://developers.openai.com/codex/subagents>
- OpenAI Codex configuration reference: <https://developers.openai.com/codex/config-reference>
- OpenAI Codex CLI docs: <https://developers.openai.com/codex/cli>
- OpenAI coding model docs: <https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5-codex>

Use this guide when a Claude skill contains inline `Agent({...})`, `Task({...})`,
or named agent routing and you need to decide whether the Codex-native result
should stay inline or become a standalone TOML agent file.

## 1. Paradigm Shift

Claude often defines delegated behavior inline:

- `Agent({...})` contains the prompt, model hint, and mode next to the call site
- `Task({...})` acts as both task tracker and delegation primitive
- named `subagent_type` values can point to file-backed Claude agents

Codex splits those concerns:

- one-off delegation stays inline via `spawn_agent(...)`
- reusable roles live as standalone TOML agent configs under `.codex/agents/`
- progress tracking is separate (`update_plan`)
- runtime limits like max depth or concurrency live in `config.toml`, not in the
  agent file itself

Decision tree:

| Source shape | Keep inline? | Create TOML agent? | Why |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| One-off prompt slice, unique to one workflow phase | Yes | No | Reuse value is low; keep intent local |
| Named reusable reviewer or diagnostic role shared across skills | No | Yes | This is a real runtime dependency |
| Thin proxy that only calls one MCP tool and returns | Usually yes | Rarely | The value is in the caller contract, not the role |
| Routing table that selects among several named roles | No | Yes, one file per role | Codex routing works best with explicit role contracts |

Migration rule:

1. If the behavior is reusable and name-worthy, create `.codex/agents/<name>.toml`.
2. If the behavior is tightly coupled to one phase, keep it inline.
3. If a routing table can emit several role names, each reachable role must have
   a real Codex agent file before the migration is considered complete.

## 2. Intent-Based Model Mapping

This section is **migration package policy**, not an official OpenAI
Claude-to-Codex conversion table.

OpenAI documents the Codex configuration surface and coding-model families, but
it does not publish a canonical mapping from Claude labels such as `sonnet` or
`haiku` to Codex runtime IDs. This package therefore maps by **agent job** and
uses the runtime names exposed in the current workspace.

Policy:

| Agent job | Typical source signal | Recommended Codex runtime | Why |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Plan discovery, file lookup, status parsing | tiny prompt, deterministic parsing | `gpt-5.4-mini` + `medium` | Cheap and fast, but still reliable on structured text |
| Diff summarizer or commit-message helper | reads a small diff and extracts lessons | `gpt-5.4-mini` + `medium` | Fast class is enough when the task is bounded |
| Focused implementer inside one module | single bounded context, direct edits | `gpt-5.4` + `medium` | Default coding worker for normal implementation depth |
| Orchestrator helper that correlates several files | multi-file reasoning, but not adversarial | `gpt-5.4` + `medium` | Stronger coordination without paying the high-reasoning cost everywhere |
| Adversarial reviewer, diagnostic specialist, architecture critic | failure analysis, hidden edge cases | `gpt-5.4` + `high` | Expensive thinking is justified when the role is risk-focused |
| High-volume batch worker with strict per-item contracts | many small independent jobs | `gpt-5.3-codex-spark` + `high` | Good fit for high-throughput, tool-heavy micro-tasks |
| Parent-session mirror role | source says `inherit` | omit override | Keep the parent session in control |

Heuristics:

- Map by job first, not by old Claude model name.
- Only override `model_reasoning_effort` when the workflow truly needs it.
- Preserve `inherit` when the parent already chose the right runtime.
- Prefer one project-wide policy table over ad hoc per-skill guesses.

Cross-reference:

- `agent-field-mapping.md` covers Claude frontmatter to Codex agent output, with TOML as the native target.
- This section is the authoritative source for embedded sub-agent conversion.

## 3. Custom Agent TOML File Schema

The OpenAI configuration reference distinguishes between **per-agent files** and
**global orchestration settings**. Do not mix them.

### 3.1 Per-Agent File Fields

Required fields:

| Field | Required | Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `name` | Yes | Human-readable role name |
| `description` | Yes | Short selection guidance for the parent |
| `developer_instructions` | Yes | The actual behavioral contract |

Optional fields:

| Field | Optional | Use when |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `model` | Yes | The role should pin a concrete runtime |
| `model_reasoning_effort` | Yes | The role consistently needs `low` / `medium` / `high` / `xhigh` |
| `sandbox_mode` | Yes | The role needs a fixed sandbox (`read-only` or `workspace-write`) |
| `mcp_servers` | Yes | Use only with explicit `[mcp_servers.<id>]` config tables |
| `skills.config` | Yes | Represent as repeated `[[skills.config]]` entries with `path` and `enabled` |
| `nickname_candidates` | Yes | Nice-to-have display names for spawned agents |

Minimal template:

```toml
name = "Example Agent B"
description = "Review changed code for bugs, regressions, and missing tests."
developer_instructions = """
Return findings first, ordered by severity.
Do not propose speculative issues without evidence from the diff.
"""
```

Expanded example:

```toml
name = "Plan Discovery"
description = "Resolve a workflow plan identifier and return structured plan metadata."
sandbox_mode = "read-only"
developer_instructions = """
Read matching plan status memory first.
Resolve the matching detailed plan record.
Return the exact structured discovery contract expected by the parent workflow.
"""
model = "gpt-5.4-mini"
model_reasoning_effort = "medium"

[[skills.config]]
path = ".codex/skills/example-skill"
enabled = true

nickname_candidates = ["Scout", "Locator"]
```

### 3.2 Global `config.toml` Settings

These settings belong in user-level or project-level `config.toml`, not in the
individual agent file:

| Key | Scope | Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `agents.max_threads` | Global | Maximum parallel agent jobs |
| `agents.max_depth` | Global | Maximum recursive spawn depth |
| `agents.job_max_runtime_seconds` | Global | Runtime cap for spawned jobs |

Design rule:

- Put role behavior in the agent file.
- Put orchestration ceilings in `config.toml`.

## 4. Before -> After Examples

These examples use representative source locations so the migration remains
auditable instead of illustrative-only.

### Example A: `example-skill` DISCOVER phase

Source:

- `.codex/skills/example-skill/references/workflow-mode.md` — `DISCOVER Stage`

Claude/Codex-inline shape today:

```text
spawn_agent("<plan_discovery_role>", {
  role: "Plan discovery specialist",
  prompt: "Find the plan matching this argument ..."
})
```

Codex custom-agent shape:

```toml
# .codex/agents/<plan_discovery_role>.toml
name = "Plan Discovery"
description = "Resolve a workflow plan identifier and return structured plan metadata."
sandbox_mode = "read-only"
developer_instructions = """
Read the workflow handoff artifact first.
Resolve the matching detailed plan record.
Return the structured discovery payload expected by the parent workflow.
"""
model = "gpt-5.4-mini"
model_reasoning_effort = "medium"
```

Why this one deserves a TOML file:

- It is reusable.
- It has a stable output contract.
- Several orchestrators may need the same plan-resolution role.

### Example B: named reviewer dependency

Source:

- an existing reviewer role under `.claude/agents/`

Before:

```text
subagent_type: "<review_specialist>"
```

After:

```toml
# .codex/agents/<review_specialist>.toml
name = "Example Agent B"
description = "Review code changes for defects, regressions, and missing tests."
sandbox_mode = "read-only"
developer_instructions = """
Return findings first, ordered by severity.
Ground every finding in concrete code evidence.
Mention residual risk if testing coverage is incomplete.
"""
model = "gpt-5.4"
model_reasoning_effort = "high"
```

Why:

- This is a named, reusable role.
- It is a dependency edge, not an inline prompt slice.

### Example C: `example-commit-helper` fast helper

Source:

- `.codex/skills/example-commit-helper/SKILL.md`

Before:

```text
Sparse sessions: a lightweight reviewer reads the diff and summarizes code-derived context
```

After:

```toml
# .codex/agents/<commit_diff_summarizer>.toml
name = "Commit Diff Summarizer"
description = "Read a staged diff and add 1-2 code-grounded learned bullets."
sandbox_mode = "read-only"
developer_instructions = """
Read only the provided diff.
Add concise code-derived lessons.
Do not restate the entire commit message.
"""
model = "gpt-5.4-mini"
model_reasoning_effort = "medium"
```

Why:

- The job is narrow and opinionated.
- It is reusable across commit workflows.

### Example D: plan-review Gemini proxy

Source:

- a plan-review prompt reference under `.codex/skills/`

Before:

```text
Thin proxy agent whose only job is: call an external large-context reviewer, then return its result
```

Recommended Codex result:

```text
Keep this inline with spawn_agent(...)
```

Why this should stay inline:

- The value is in the parent workflow contract, not in a standalone reusable role.
- It is a one-tool proxy with plan-specific persistence behavior.
- Turning every thin proxy into a file-backed agent creates role sprawl without
  increasing reuse.

## 5. Best Practices

Derived from Codex configuration and operational behavior:

- Keep custom agents narrow and opinionated. A good agent file captures one role,
  not an entire workflow.
- Default `agents.max_depth` to `1` unless a role truly needs recursive
  delegation. Recursive spawning is the fastest way to lose control of context
  and cost.
- Prefer parallel reads over parallel writes. Independent discovery workers are
  cheap; concurrent writers need explicit file-boundary ownership.
- Call `close_agent` when a long-lived worker is no longer needed.
- Make sub-agents return summaries or structured payloads, not raw transcripts.
- Keep stable contracts in TOML files and volatile task details in the parent
  prompt.
- Avoid role explosion. If a role is only used once and has no reuse value, keep
  it inline.

Context rule:

- Custom agents should reduce context pollution. Put stable role policy in the
  agent file so the parent prompt only has to send task-specific facts.

## 6. `spawn_agents_on_csv` Batch Pattern

When migrating many embedded agent sites, treat it as structured batch work.

Suggested CSV columns:

```csv
source_path,site_id,decision,target_agent,job_class,notes
examples/hello-world-skill/SKILL.md,discover,custom-agent,<plan_discovery_role>,discovery,reusable structured output
.codex/skills/<review_skill>/references/review-prompts.md,model-proxy,inline,,proxy,keep inline
```

Recommended worker contract:

1. Parent reads the CSV.
2. Parent spawns one worker per row.
3. Each worker decides inline vs TOML using Sections 1-3 of this guide.
4. Each worker returns one structured result object.
5. Parent merges the results and writes the final migration summary.

Structured result shape:

```json
{
  "source_path": "examples/hello-world-skill/SKILL.md",
  "site_id": "discover",
  "decision": "custom-agent",
  "target_agent": ".codex/agents/<plan_discovery_role>.toml",
  "model": "gpt-5.4-mini",
  "reasoning_effort": "medium",
  "notes": "Reusable structured plan lookup role"
}
```

Why CSV works well here:

- each site is independently reviewable
- parallel workers can operate safely when the write targets do not overlap
- the parent can enforce a single output schema instead of free-form agent prose
